item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So it's basically a nexus?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Good worldbuilding, though it feels like there's something missing. Some broader significance, some threat to humanity. Otherwise, it just feels like a parallel-universe organization with similar principles to the Foundation.

Not sure if I'm allowed to edit old articles, so here's a typo.

The remains of a massive unknown organism, much of which currently serving as the framework of the Imperial Palace.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, the Foundation counterpart was founded in 632 CE. Is that a random date to show how far back they go? Or is there a connection to baseline history? This side of the crevasse, that is the year Muhammad gave his final sermon, and passed away, and Muslim schisms were established, complete with wars in that same year.

Not that I can think of any connection, but that's a thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love the concept but agree with those who say it's a bit too High Fantasy. It doesn't seem as anomalous or intriguing as it should, coming off a little too shiny while not delivering the edge I enjoy in SCP's.

Who is the empress? How many arms does she have? I suggest that she be the least 'human' of all, or perhaps the most. A mundane heart to this fantasy empire might be interesting, or a reality bender fulfilling her dream with an elaborate Antarctic takeover.

item.title: Glad to be the 300th upvote
item.content:encoded:
This is some real interesting worldbuilding, here. This site needs more worldbuilding like this.

+1

item.title: Typo
item.content:encoded:
Someone might have already pointed this out, but there's an error here:

SCP-1483-1b: “The Black Court” – A subspecies of SCP-1483-1, specialized to survive outside in winter conditions by means of a thick layer of body fat and fur. Incapable of reproducing with other clades. SCP-1483b traditionally takes over most facets of public life, including the senate, military, and administrative positions, during the winter months. Summer months are spent in nomadic communes.

The "SCP-1483b" in the middle should be "SCP-1483-1b," unless I have completely misunderstood what this paragraph is saying and the cloud-portals that unleash psycho cannibals are the ones that run things in the winter.

Other than that, good job on a very extensively thought-out SCP. It's good to know there's at least one alternate universe that's not actively trying to kill us.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My first thought was of Green Antarctica :D
Vote +200 is the continent of this-is-incredibly-cool.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love how in-depth this thing is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I do love a good bit of worldbuilding. Good work, Djoric. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Reading through this again in preparation to diving into the AE canon and enjoying it immensely.

I made a few edits for typos and minor grammar, and also a reasonably major semantic fix around the description of b, -1c, and -1f.

Previously, it indicated that b was a dark cloud and spawned instances of -1c. There was also a sentence about -1f consuming everything in their path that was somewhat isolated in the middle of the paragraph. Further down, in the description of -1f, they are referred to as -1c at one point in a sentence in a way which didn't make sense.

I altered it to indicate that b, the cloud, also spawns a number of -1fs with it, which then consume everything in their path. In the -1f description, I changed the -1c reference to -1f.

Please feel free to revert if this is incorrect, but I am pretty certain it is right.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Whoops. *rushes off to fix something.*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Aye. It has been fixed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think it's supposed to say "broad".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What is a "borad build"? The only definition I can find anywhere is on Urban Dictionary, which is not the most reputable source. And that sentence seems to be unfinished regardless.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You know, after thinking about it for a bit,I'm getting quite tired of the whole grimderp Foundation thing that's been going on recently, my downvote is now an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can see where you're coming from, but there's a limit to the stupidity I am willing to characterize the Foundation with (even when I characterize them as blundering idiots the majority of the time) and them putting on their Christian Bale Batman voice and going "We need to kill all the Antarcticans for no raisin." is well beyond that limit.

They've got an advanced civilization that is willing to humor their study, and the Antarcticans (mostly) don't give a shit about the Foundation world, because they're filthy primitive goat-fucking northerners.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm afraid I have to -1 this. In my headcanon, the SCP Foundation would absolutely not negotiate at all with entities from another world in any capacity, besides warning them to stay the hell away from our world or be annihilated. Their extent of the interaction would be securing the portal to 1483, and killing or capturing anything that came from the other side besides their own expeditions.

Once the war inevitably got started, the Foundation would win by tossing every XK-class keter SCP that needs disposing into the portal and watching the mayhem unfold.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's no relation between Imperial and baseline languages beyond that which would be considered convergent evolution.

First contact was based almost entirely upon pantomimes, and they worked from there. Issues existed, but were minor, because both parties were generally smart enough to not make any sudden moves and fuck everything up. The learning was a mutual thing, with both parties learning the other language, and so the cooperation really smoothed things over.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Considering how long ago evolution in this world diverged from our it seems strange to me that there is no mention of their language other than that it has been standardized. Is their language related to any baseline languages? Did the foundation learn their language, or teach them some of one or more of ours, or both? Did a lack of a common language cause any issues during first contact? Or did they pick up some baseline language from previous visitors to their universe that made communication a little easier?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I shall address the crevasse thing.

To answer your questions:

1) The Empire treats the Foundation as a curiosity, with said treatment ranging from "interesting new species of gecko" to "those salt of the earth people down the road who are all right but a bit off" to "child with developmental issues". This is all basically limited to where the Foundation is: big cities in the central provinces. There are many in the Empire who don't even know they exist.

The official party line is that the Foundation are "travelers from afar", which means a great deal of things.

2) No, there was no cross-colonization. Evolution was convergent up to a certain point of divergence, it's just a lot tougher to trace where a lot of the animals and plants come from, especially with some meddling involved. I'll see what I can do to smooth that section out.

3) Oh, the Empire has many, many secrets. One of which is what you mentioned. The original Empire was named so because it was the first one to unify the continent. The fact that this is the Third means that this has not been a continuous thing…yet the records don't show any evidence of this.

Imperial censors are really good at their job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just read it again after your updates. Can't wait to see the upcomin Tale. Only one specific comment: ''small crevasse'' is a bit vague. What is the biggest thing that could pass through? Could the doorway be made wider by digging up around it?

The following is mostly a bunch of things I am wondering about. Food for thought.

What does the Foundation means to the Empire and what do they know about our world? I'm curious about how the Foundation went about making an agreement with a whole empire.

If I got this right, plants and animal share no common ancestor with our world, but people do, so one of the people probably colonized the other side at some time in the past.

“The dealings of savages and their rotting lands are of no concern to us. Do not waste your time in asking after them.”

I think they're hiding something. Why is it called an Empire, rather than a unified kingdom? Do they have dominion over other continents that they don't want to talk about?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Did a few tweaks and updates.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As many others have said, very, very nice. upvote.
But you left out any description of the morphological differences between h sapiens and h antarcticus. H heidelbergensis didn't exactly look "normal" by today's standards, and if development went in a different dirrection, even the "ordinary" SCP-1483-1 might look very, very different. You might address this in the tales (which I will definitely read), but there probably should be some note here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 'Aggressive Invasion of Baseline' is totally going to be the name of my first dance music album that I'll never make.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Although complaints about parallel universes being overdone are frequent, I actually quite like the idea of having various kinds of those. This one specially is well fleshed-out without being overwelming. That and it's great potential for tales. Now, not that my upvote would mean anything by this point, but there you go, take it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Heh. Looks like it's broken 100. Awesome.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really enjoyed that phrase too, but I was quickly disappointed as it became apparent that this was a parallel universe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's exactly what I was going to comment, and the reason I downvoted.

It's not bad, it just seems like a bit too much for a single SCP, and a bit too "fantasy".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I totally want to visit Alternate Antarctica now…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would have upvoted for the Containment Procedures alone. The rest was just icing on the cake.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Djoric, you always manage to make something that leaves me satisfied and wondering how you come up with this shit. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very nice indeed. A good departure point. A drift from standard SCP Foundation tone, but not everything that needs to be contained needs to be creepy or depressing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You're more than welcome to headcanon Strike Team Iota-15's nickname as "The Shoggoth Stompers".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You know, when I read At the Mountains of Madness by Lovecraft, I was hoping for something like this. And now you've made it.
Thank you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'll be writing some tales regarding it, though since numerous people have already expressed interest, eh, why not. Though you may want to wait a bit until I get a tale or two out, for illustrative purposes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So do you care if other people write tales about this or…?

I'm not sure if you're going to be writing about this or anything, but I just want to be clear on whether or not you're okay with other people writing tales on something you just pushed out. I know I'd love to give it a shot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Take all of my upvotes. Just take them!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't think this is good because of the actual content, but for the actual content's potential for great tales…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very nice. The quality of the writing in this piece is really a step above. I also like the fact that you did not reveal everything there is to know about this place; the fact that some mystery still exists there is very enticing. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I never thought Antarctica would be an SCP…
Once again, I have underestimated the craziness of this wiki. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't really see it, but it's the best case scenario of a fantasy turned into a scip, so +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it. It seems exactly like how the Foundation would treat a fantasy world, with labeling and embassies.

Something about the formatting just seems…off, though. +1 nonetheless.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This needs many tales. Just, so many. One might even say all of the tales.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ditto. I saw that it had positive votes, and I read that phrase, and my first reaction was a combination of anticipation and joy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Edited a bit, addressed Abodos' and (most of) volmarius' points.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like all the implications stemming from this SCP's transition point having been left on its own for quite a while, meaning that some things might have already crept into our world from their's. I also got some slight chills reading the part about Dr. Edith Yard dying of respiratory infection, implying that she got infected from some pathogen native to this alternate Antarctica.

One little nitpick: at one point, Djoric, you note that SCP-1483 consists primarily of a central "desert." Technically, due to the lack of precipitation, the real life Antarctica is a desert. The largest desert in the world, in fact. But it's not the hot, sandy tropical desert that people typically associate with the word, which I'm assuming is what you're getting at with SCP-1483 (what with the lakes, rivers and vegetation implying that it's not the frozen desert of the real Antarctica). Maybe change up the part of the description concerning the central desert in order to take into account that distinction.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love things that are really different, and this is that! An entire land is very interesting material for now. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As Von Pincier, it's a really clever idea for the foundation to be containing an anomaly via conventional diplomacy. So that alone is worth an upvote.

Next, I like the idea of a separate world which is comparable to our own in scale of cultural development, and not in the middle of a class-XK event. So, if I could upvote twice, I would.

After that we have the other ideas this could spawn. If knowledge of anomalies is common there, does anyone recognize copies of stuff the foundation has locked up? Does Dr. Wondertainment or The Factory sell their products openly? What are the laws regarding civil rights of those SCP-effected like the Little Misters™ or SCP-085? Has our foundation ever tried launching an airplane mission up north form their base in the other word to see what the rest of the world is like? If so, what did they find?

FOR THE LOVE OF THE BROKEN GOD SOMEONE WRITE THIS!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One obviously could write up a lot more about the Antarctic Empire, but I like that Djoric was able, in one page, to flesh it out enough to be interesting and give a pretty good outline of what's going on.

Maybe some tales could expand on it. I'm sure Lord Blackwood has been over there at some point.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have to agree with anqxyr. I think that there's a good kernel here, but there needs to be way more detail for an SCP as ambitious in scope as this one. There's a foundation embassy established for twelve years, but there's only only information about a few "anomolous" subtypes of this scp.

Where's the more accessible general information? What's the capital city? Are there cities? We know that it's an empire with an empress, but who is the empress? Is she a "typical" human here or is she abnormal as well? The technology is "roughly" analogous to ours, but are there any major differences? Has there been any sort of information/technology sharing? For example, satellites are a Big Deal for our civilization, but Antarctica is about the worst place for launching spacecraft and having satellite coverage. Does this civilization bother? If they have satellites, what do they actually know of the world outside (despite being typically insular)

Hell, how have the people of SCP-1483 treated the existence of the foundation, and the existence of another (nominally) friendly plane of existence? Is their existence considered a secret similar to how the foundation would keep secret SCP-1483, or are they generally accepted? Is the foundation "embassy" itself a secret?

It really feels like an entry as ambitious in scope as this one needs to be significantly fleshed out. Compare this with the entries for SCP-093, another "parallel world" entry, where the foundation has a very limited amount of information due to the sheer danger involved in travelling there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ahh, I missed that, that fits much better. I am now drawing little hearts with my name and "SCP-1483" written in them on my Algebra II notebook. Thank you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The major divergences are because of their own scip influences. 1483-1b is basically just big and rather hairy, and the difference between 1483-1 and homo sapiens is primarily genetic, rather than in appearance.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't love this, because the divergence between our physical form and theirs seems too pronounced for the time spans mentioned. I am, however, in serious like with it due to the everything else about it, and am considering asking it to the prom.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well-written, thought out, weird in the right places. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fixed.

The majority population is 1483-1 and 1483-1b. The percentages you see vary depending on what time of year it is. There were going to be more clades mentioned in the original draft, but that was overdoing it. As with the scips, there are more, but they aren't big enough to be mentioned here, or haven't been studied yet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree with Bright to the extent that as soon as I was finished reading, I expected there to be an epic "Golden Compass"-like adventure that would span several 250 page novels. Personally I feel a lot of that's due to the detail on the subspecies of 1483-1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is good. +1

SCP-1483-1e are reported by locals as having received “all blessings” of SCP-1483b.

Is this a typo? I am not sure. From what I understand, it's SCP-1483c who grant the blessings.

What's the general population of the Empire looks like? Is it a mix of many clades, or is there some sort of "Peasant" clade? If there is, why isn't it mentioned in the second addendum?

I like that we know nothing about what is going on in the rest of the world. For all we know, it's more technologically and culturally advance than Empire, and they just have some major religious disagreements.

I like the universal overlap scale too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's still the feeling it gives.
